utterances: Oh, yeah.
We got the seven-day tube pass.
The only time…
That's critical.
…we ate out - huh?
That's critical, yeah, with the tube pass.
Yeah.
And the only times we ate out were, really, lunch.
And…
And then you can go to the pubs and eat for $12 a good hot meal.
The pub food is not as embarrassingly, you know, mushy piece as it used to be.
They've got crisp vegetables and take pride in their food, but you're eating in pub instead of a fancy restaurant.
I do want to remind you that Europe is really getting automated, and tourists are attacked from a price point of view because we don't know - we're kind of clueless and not very savvy.

But if you take the passes, like Cris got, you will save a lot money and you want to just, kind of, prepare yourself to got like the locals do and then not be subsidizing their infrastructure by doing it like the clueless tourists who would pay, you know, $5 for a bus ride.
Well, for the cost of four bus rides, you can get a two-day pass.
Mm-hmm.
We also went to the grocery store on the way back from the tube when we were headed back to our hotel.
And we would get enough food for the evening for 7 pounds.
Hmm.
That's not so bad - about $14 or so.
Cris, thanks…
But, of course, you're going to the grocery store and picnicking, but you got to turn that into a plus by choosing an atmospheric place to enjoy your picnic in front of…
Oh, yeah.
…(unintelligible) Cathedral and enjoy good local food.
I mean, see this is the new way that Americans need to travel, like people who have been relatively less wealthy have always traveled.
You know, we're just not on the top anymore because of our - the way we're running our economy.
So the reality is we got less buying power compared to the Danes and the Germans and the Brits, and it's always going to be that way.
So, let's travel smarter, but let's not just stay home because of this.
Exactly.
Cris, thanks very much for the call.
Thank you.

We did.
And here's an e-mail from Liz(ph), writing about the Island of Elba -mostly associated with Napoleon, I suppose.
But she writes: Interested in planning a holiday on 2009 to celebrate my dad's 70th birthday.
His grandparents came from there in 1912.
A group of 10 or so family members will probably go.
What would you recommend to make plans with the family, a group of that size?
Do you recommend finding a home or staying at hotels?
And the best way to travel from the island to the mainland?
Well, there's good ferry connections between the mainland and Elba.
That's no problem.
I'll remind our listeners that the Europeans love their islands.
And I'd generally leave the islands for the Europeans - some of them are resorts.
And you just don't want to compete with the densely populated continent of lemming sun worshipers like Europe is.
I'd rather stay in the mainland, in general.
But in the case of Liz she's got relatives.
Her family heritage and so on, so Elba would make sense.
My son is with a group right now, a group of his fellow students.
They took a break from their studying in Rome and went down to Sicily.
And Andy(ph) knew the smartness of renting an apartment.
So he had eight or 10 of his students sharing an apartment, and - for a week.
And they had their own kitchen.
And it was really much, much as what half the price of a cheap hotel to have 10 people sharing very comfortable apartment accommodation.
So, if you can stay put and if you have a group in, you can consume smartly there and enjoy sort of the economy of scale.
And I find all over Europe with -given the high cost of hotels, there are more alternatives like apartments that people are taking advantage of.
Let's talk with Peter(ph).
Peter, with us from Wichita in Kansas.
Hi.
Go ahead, Peter.
Oh, my wife and I are planning a trip to Spain, to Barcelona specifically, in May.
Do you have any recommendations?
We're on about a $5,000-budget?
Does that include…
That includes airfare.
I was going to say.
Go ahead.
Well, I would say what - you know, that's the question.
What does it cost to travel in Europe?
I would say if you have a - if there's two adults traveling together and you have $150 a day per person for room and board, you got it made.
That's very reasonable.
That's very realistic anywhere in Europe, $300 per day, per couple for room and board.
Of course, you've got transportation after that and your sightseeing.
The kids are wiggling.
Everyone is smiling.
The judge nods.
Barcelona is an expensive place from an accommodation's point of view.
It's a wonderful place from an eating point of view and from a sightseeing point of view.
Barcelona is one of the cities to me that has a little less - a little more demand for hotels, and they got rooms.
So you want to make a reservation well in advance, Peter, for that.
But I absolutely love Barcelona.
And I don't know all the details of your budget - $5,000, you have to take your flight out of that and so on.
But I just think that if you figure $150 a day, when two people to come together, one person - it's more expensive, because a single room cost almost as much as a double room.
So, two people - the more people you pack in the room, obviously the cheaper it gets.
Let me quickly review these comparative prices for you.
Double rooms range - it might chart all over Europe from 80 to $130 with breakfast in a simple hotel, in a medium hotel from 120 to $220 for double in that way.
If you look at the cheapest bunks, if you're hosteling - and there's $2,000 hostels around Europe.
They took youth out of the old system.
If you're over 55, you get a discount and a membership card.
Bunks and youth hostels range from 20 to $40 a night.
A great thing about that, especially if your younger, hostel there's - you have a kitchen there and you got friends from around the world to plan tomorrow's adventures with.
The cost of a Big Mac ranges from $3 to $6 depending on the country.
So, it's, you know, it's $3 in Vienna, it's $3 in Paris and Rome, it'd be $5 in Munich and $6 in Scandinavia.
A one-hour train ride out of any town in Europe ranges from, I'd say $6 to $25; $6 dollars for an hour of train ride in Eastern Europe and Greece, $25 for a train ride in Britain or Ireland.
So, you can see, it ranges a lot from country to country.
You'll spend 10 or $15 for major museum admissions.
Peter, good luck.
Thank you very much.
Bye-bye.
Thank you.
